creational_patterns: Singleton - Ensures only one instance of a class exists throughout the application, useful for resource managers like databases
Factory - Creates objects without specifying their exact classes, allowing flexibility in object creation
Builder - Constructs complex objects step by step, separating construction from representation
Prototype - Creates new objects by cloning existing ones rather than creating from scratch
Abstract Factory - Creates families of related objects without specifying their concrete classes
structural_patterns: Adapter - Converts one interface into another that clients expect, making incompatible interfaces work together
Decorator - Adds behavior to objects dynamically without modifying their class, using composition over inheritance
Facade - Provides simplified interface to complex subsystems, hiding complexity behind simple methods
Proxy - Provides surrogate or placeholder for another object, controlling access and adding additional behavior
Bridge - Decouples abstraction from implementation so they can vary independently
Composite - Composes objects into tree structures to represent whole-part hierarchies
behavioral_patterns: Observer - Notifies multiple objects about state changes automatically, implementing publish-subscribe mechanism
Strategy - Encapsulates algorithms that can be swapped, allowing runtime algorithm selection
Command - Encapsulates requests as objects, allowing queuing and undo/redo functionality
State - Allows objects to change behavior based on internal state without conditional logic
Template Method - Defines algorithm skeleton in base class, letting subclasses override specific steps
Chain of Responsibility - Passes request along chain of handlers until one handles it
Visitor - Represents operation to perform on object structure elements without changing their classes
pattern_benefits: Code Reusability - Design patterns provide proven solutions that can be applied to similar problems
Maintainability - Patterns make code easier to understand, modify, and extend for other developers
Scalability - Well-designed patterns help systems grow without major refactoring
Communication - Patterns provide common vocabulary for developers to discuss design decisions
Performance - Patterns can be optimized once and benefit all code using them
